html:
Pharaoh saved at death — but also drowned Contradiction Moderate Quran 10:90–92 vs 28:40
"...we save you in body that you may be to those who succeed you a sign." (10:92)
"So We took him and his soldiers and threw them into the sea..." (28:40)

What the verses say

In 10:90–92, Pharaoh confesses Islam as he drowns and Allah announces his body will be preserved as a sign. Other verses (28:40, 7:136, 43:55) describe Pharaoh as drowned and destroyed.

Why this is a problem

Modern Muslim apologists cite Ramesses II's preserved mummy as the fulfillment of 10:92's preservation claim. But mummification was standard practice for all Egyptian pharaohs — it requires no miracle and proves nothing. The verse says "We will save you in body" as if uniquely preserved, but every major Pharaoh was mummified. Pharaoh's deathbed conversion also sits awkwardly with the Quran's own principle that repentance at death's moment is not accepted (4:18): either his last-moment faith counted (contradicting 4:18) or it didn't (making the body-preservation gesture arbitrary).

Why it fails

The "Pharaoh's mummy" apologetic is weak: Ramesses II's preservation is standard Egyptian funerary practice, not divine exception. The verse presents the preservation as a unique sign, distinguishing it from all other Pharaohs — but they were all mummified. Retrofitting a standard cultural practice as Quranic miracle is the shape of retroactive reading, not genuine prediction.

Mountains as pegs holding down the earth Science Claims Moderate Quran 78:6–7 (also 16:15, 21:31)
"Have We not made the earth a resting place? And the mountains as stakes?" (78:6–7)
"And He placed within the earth firmly set mountains, lest it should shift with you..." (16:15)

What the verses say

Mountains function as awtad (stakes, pegs) driven into the earth to prevent it from shaking.

Why this is a problem

In actual geology, mountains do not prevent earthquakes — most mountain ranges are formed by tectonic plate collisions and are ongoing producers of seismic activity. The Himalayas continue to rise because the Indian plate pushes into the Eurasian plate, generating earthquakes in the process. Earth's stability derives from gravitational dynamics and angular momentum, not mountain pegs. The Quranic picture reflects ancient Near Eastern cosmology: a flat earth held stable by weights.

Why it fails

The isostasy retrofit reads 20th-century geology back into a 7th-century text that reads naturally as flat-earth cosmology. Quran 16:15 says mountains were set to keep the earth from "shaking with you" — but mountains cause earthquakes; they do not prevent them. No classical commentator extracted the isostasy claim before modern geology made it available. Scientific miracles identified only after the science settles are compatibility-after-the-fact, not prediction.

The sun runs to a fixed resting place Science Claims Moderate Quran 36:38
"And the sun runs [on course] toward its stopping point. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing."

What the verse says

The sun moves through the sky toward a fixed stopping place (mustaqarr — a place of settling or rest).

Why this is a problem

The sun does not move across the sky; the apparent solar motion is caused by Earth's rotation. Classical tafsir and the hadith tradition understood this verse as describing the sun's nightly journey to rest beneath Allah's throne, prostrate and asking permission to rise again (Bukhari 3199) — geocentric mythology, not astronomy. Modern apologists reinterpret the verse as referring to the sun's galactic orbit. But this requires reading "stopping point" as "continuous circumgalactic motion," which is its opposite; no classical commentator held this view; and it directly contradicts the hadith explanation that Muslims consider authoritative.

Why it fails

The galactic-motion reading is pure retrofit. Classical tafsir read "run" in geocentric terms; the "resting place" was the sun's nightly retreat under Allah's throne. Modern apologists read modern astronomy back into the verse; classical readers could not, because the galactic framework did not exist. This is the standard i'jaz 'ilmi pattern: compatibility identified after the science settles.

Creation in six days — or eight? A day-count contradiction Contradiction Science Claims Moderate Quran 7:54 vs 41:9–12
"Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and earth in six days..." (7:54)
"...the earth in two days... mountains and sustenance in four days... then heaven in two days..." (41:9–12)

What the verses say

Most passages say creation took six days. But 41:9–12 gives a breakdown: 2 days for the earth, 4 days for mountains and sustenance, and 2 days for the heavens — totalling 8.

Why this is a problem

Classical commentators were aware of this and proposed that the "four days" includes the prior "two days" — making the total 2+2+2=6 rather than 2+4+2=8. But this requires reading "four days" as meaning "two more days after the first two," which is not how counting works in ordinary language. The natural reading of three sequential periods is sequential addition. A divine revelation should not require arithmetic reinterpretation to avoid self-contradiction in a passage describing the most fundamental act of creation.

Why it fails

The "overlapping count" reading is rescue logic, not natural Arabic reading of sequential numbers. And the "phase not arithmetic" move makes the numbers informationally empty while conceding the natural reading produces a contradiction. A divine text whose self-described clarity is undermined by its own arithmetic within a single creation passage has an editorial problem the tradition has worked hard to paper over.

Noah's flood covered the earth — with water spouting from ovens Science Claims Strange / Obscure Moderate Quran 11:40, 23:27
"Until, when Our command came and the oven overflowed [with water], We said, 'Load upon it [i.e., the ark] of each [creature] two mates and your family...'" (11:40)

What the verse says

Noah's flood began when a specific clay bread oven (al-tannur) started overflowing with water. The flood then drowned all humanity except Noah's family.

Why this is a problem

Two problems compound. First, the image of a flood beginning from a household oven is bizarre — classical commentators debated endlessly whether this was literal or metaphorical, which itself signals the text's lack of clarity. Second, the Quran endorses a global flood that modern geology, genetics, archaeology, and anthropology comprehensively contradict. Civilizations in China, Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Americas maintained continuous records across the period when Noah's flood would have destroyed them. Human genetics show no population bottleneck corresponding to eight survivors a few thousand years ago.

Why it fails

The variety of interpretations for the tannur is itself evidence the text's meaning was unclear from the earliest period — which contradicts the Quran's claim to clarity. And a global flood has been comprehensively ruled out by multiple independent scientific disciplines. The only way to reconcile the Quran with evidence is to read Noah's flood as a local Mesopotamian event, which concedes the Quran is not a reliable global historical source.

Iblis the jinn refuses to prostrate — but the command was given to the angels Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 2:34, 18:50
"And [mention] when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate before Adam'; so they prostrated, except for Iblees..." (2:34)
"...and they prostrated, except for Iblees. He was of the jinn and departed from [i.e., disobeyed] the command of his Lord..." (18:50)

What the verses say

In 2:34, Iblees is listed as an exception among the angels commanded to prostrate to Adam. In 18:50, the Quran clarifies he was actually a jinn, not an angel.

Why this is a problem

If Iblees was a jinn and not an angel, then the command given to the angels was not addressed to him — his refusal is not disobedience, because he was outside the order's addressees. The text of 2:34 implies he was an angel; 18:50 then corrects this, which is itself an admission that the earlier text was imprecise. A related problem: the Allah who elsewhere forbids prostration to anything but Him here commands every angel to prostrate to a creature — and uses the same verb (sajada) as for worship, requiring classical commentators to scramble to distinguish prostration-of-respect from prostration-of-worship, a distinction the text itself does not draw.

Why it fails

This reading is not in the text: 2:34 presents him as part of the addressed group; 18:50 then corrects the reader's assumption. The correction is the evidence of the problem. The Quran patches its own imprecision rather than presenting a consistent account from the outset.

A crow teaches Cain how to bury Abel — lifted from Jewish midrash Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Moderate Quran 5:31
"Then Allah sent a crow searching [i.e., scratching] in the ground to show him how to hide the disgrace of his brother. He said, 'O woe to me! Have I failed to be like this crow and hide the disgrace [i.e., body] of my brother?'"

What the verse says

After Cain kills Abel, Allah sends a crow to demonstrate burial by scratching in dirt. Cain watches and learns to bury the body.

Why this is a problem

This story does not appear in the Hebrew Bible. It comes from Jewish rabbinical literature — specifically the Pirke de-Rabbi Eliezer (chapter 21) and the Jerusalem Talmud (Sanhedrin 4:5) — texts composed centuries before the Quran and circulating in oral form in 7th-century Arabia. The crow-burial motif is a distinctly rabbinical midrash: imaginative embellishment on the biblical narrative, explicitly creative rather than historical. A divine author transmitting the true account of history should not reproduce the imaginative glosses of later Jewish teachers as fact.

Why it fails

This requires accepting the reliability of rabbinical literature that Islam elsewhere rejects as corrupted (tahrif). The move cuts both ways: if the midrash is reliable here, a great deal of rabbinical interpretation Islam rejects becomes authoritative; if it is not, the Quran is reproducing known legend as revelation.

Amputate the hand of the thief — regardless of circumstance Treatment of Disbelievers Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 5:38
"[As for] the thief, the male and the female, amputate their hands in recompense for what they earned [i.e., committed] as a deterrent [punishment] from Allah. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise."

What the verse says

The penalty for theft is hand amputation. The verse specifies no minimum value stolen, no consideration of need or poverty, no distinction between first offense and habitual criminal. Later juristic elaboration added those conditions; the Quran itself does not.

Why this is a problem

Permanent mutilation for a property crime is disproportionate by modern legal standards. The harm done (the stolen goods, potentially of minimal value) is punished with permanent, visible, career-ending mutilation — a cost compounding across decades, with no proportionality to the original offense. Hand amputation is still actively enforced under Sharia law in Saudi Arabia, Iran, Sudan, and northern Nigeria on the literal reading of this verse, demonstrating that the claim Islam is compatible with modern human rights requires directly confronting a criminal code still being enforced in 2025.

Why it fails

These mitigations are defensible but come from juristic reasoning, not from the verse itself, which is unconditional. The need for 1,400 years of scholarly elaboration to make the verse humane is an admission that the verse, on its face, is not.

Jews and polytheists are "most intense in animosity" toward believers Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Quran 5:82
"You will surely find the most intense of the people in animosity toward the believers [to be] the Jews and those who associate others with Allah; and you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, 'We are Christians.'"

What the verse says

A collective, unqualified judgment: Jews and polytheists are most hostile to Muslims; Christians are nearest in affection. The verse does not say "some Jews" or "certain factions" — it is a general present-tense observation about entire religious communities.

Why this is a problem

The judgment is both morally troubling and empirically false as a universal claim. In 1492, Ottoman Muslims received Jews expelled by Catholic Spain — the people the Quran calls "nearest in affection." Jewish physicians served Muslim caliphs for centuries. The verse is cited in modern Islamist anti-Semitic rhetoric as Quranic warrant for treating Jewish hostility as theological essence rather than historical contingency. A book claimed to be the eternal word of God should not contain collective ethnic defamation.

Why it fails

The verse's present tense ("you will find") is written as a universal observation, not a historical remark. Mainstream Islamic discourse applied it universally for 1,400 years — which is why it still shapes attitudes toward Jews in many Muslim cultures today. If the verse were historically bounded, classical tafsir would say so; it does not.

Wine is a "work of Satan" — yet paradise contains rivers of wine Contradiction Moderate Quran 5:90 vs 47:15
"...intoxicants... are but defilement from the work of Satan, so avoid it..." (5:90)
"...and rivers of wine delicious to those who drink..." (47:15, describing paradise)

What the verses say

On earth, wine (khamr) is classed with idol-worship as Satanic defilement to be avoided absolutely. In paradise, rivers of wine are among the rewards for the righteous — "delicious," causing no headache (37:47) and not intoxicating (56:19).

Why this is a problem

If wine is intrinsically Satanic, it should not appear in God's garden in any form — even purified. If it is not intrinsically Satanic, then 5:90 overstates the prohibition. The apologetic response — that paradise wine is chemically different — uses the same word (khamr) for both, making the distinction arbitrary. More revealingly, the reward's appeal to the original audience depended on it being exactly the drink they couldn't have on earth. The incentive structure undermines the moral claim: if wine were truly defilement, it should not be dangled as divine reward.

Why it fails

This resolves only the physiological issue. The Quran uses khamr for both substances; calling the same word by the same name while treating them as categorically different requires explanation the text does not provide. And if non-intoxicating wine is acceptable in paradise, the classical juristic prohibition of wine even in non-intoxicating quantities becomes incoherent.

"Allah is the best of deceivers" — divine deception as a virtue Logical Inconsistency Prophetic Character Moderate Quran 3:54, 8:30
"And they [i.e., the disbelievers] planned, but Allah planned. And Allah is the best of planners." (3:54)

What the verses say

The Arabic makr — rendered "plan" by Saheeh International — carries the primary meanings of scheme, stratagem, and deception in classical and modern Arabic. When humans do makr, it is always pejorative. The Quran uses the same root for what the disbelievers do and what Allah does, then rates Allah as superior at it.

Why this is a problem

If deception is evil when humans practice it, it is evil when God does it. If it becomes good when done skillfully, then the disbelievers' deception should also be evaluated on skill rather than condemned per se. The theological stakes are high: Christian theology worked extensively to establish that God cannot lie or deceive — because a deceiving God cannot be trusted, including in His revelations. If Allah is the best deceiver, on what basis does a Muslim trust the Quran itself to be honest?

Why it fails

The same word appears in both clauses of 3:54 — for the enemies' scheming and for Allah's response. It cannot honestly mean deception in the first clause and innocent planning in the second. The more honest reading is that the Quran is content to call Allah a superior schemer and leaves the moral implications unaddressed.

The camel through the eye of a needle — the Quran quotes Jesus without attribution Strange / Obscure Jesus / Christology Moderate Quran 7:40
"Indeed, those who deny Our verses and are arrogant toward them — the gates of Heaven will not be opened for them, nor will they enter Paradise until a camel enters into the eye of a needle."

What the verse says

Disbelievers will not enter paradise until a camel passes through the eye of a needle — a proverbial impossibility. Jesus uses the identical image in Mark 10:25, Matthew 19:24, and Luke 18:25 — six centuries earlier — to warn rich disciples about the obstacle of wealth.

Why this is a problem

The Quran takes a distinctive saying associated with Jesus and repurposes it without attribution as a general statement about disbelievers entering paradise. The simplest explanation is that Muhammad knew the phrase from Syriac Christian tradition and incorporated it. A divine author composing the eternal word would not silently borrow a teaching idiom from a historical human teacher and convert it to a different point without acknowledging the source.

Why it fails

The specific construction — "until a camel enters the eye of a needle" — is tied in the first-century Mediterranean world to Jesus (Matthew 19:24; Mark 10:25; Luke 18:25). The Quran's version arrives six centuries later in a setting where the Gospels were the dominant text preserving that phrasing. Without independent pre-Christian attestation of the exact construction, the likelier account is Quranic echo of a circulating Gospel saying.
